coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from cassava stems via acid/alkali-assisted hydrothermal carbonization: formation, mechanism and application in drug release
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Yujuan
Wang, Fei
Ma, Xiaojun
Yin, Fen
Li, Dongna
Li, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Green and sustainable carbon quantum dots from lignocellulosic biomass (LB-CQDs) have been widely used in various fields. However, the recalcitrant nature of lignocellulose leads to the low yield of LB-CQDs. In this work, we proposed an effective strategy for obtaining high yield LB-CQDs from cassava stems through acid/alkali-assist hydrothermal carbonization. The results indicated that LB-CQDs processed by HNO3 +urea (N-U-CQDs) exhibited the best fluorescence characteristics due to their smaller size and higher N content. Compared with unmodified LB-CQDs, the yield and fluorescence efficiency of N-U-CQDs increased by 36% and 80%, respectively. Meanwhile, a speculation was made on the formation route and luminescence mechanism of LB-CQDs from acid/alkali-assist hydrothermal carbonization. In addition, biomass mineral materials CaCO3 was induced by LB-CQDs to prepare carrier materials CQDs/CaCO3 for the loading and sustained-release of anticancer drug (doxorubicin, DOX). The results suggested the retained specific recognition of DOX@CQDs/CaCO3 for diseased cells. Compared to the environment simulating normal tissue with pH= 7.4, DOX@CQDs/CaCO3 exhibited faster release efficiency (52.6%, 4 h) and higher release amount (93.1%, 48 h) of loaded DOX in the environment simulating cancer cells with pH= 5.0. Therefore, it offered a novel strategy for the efficient conversion of lignocellulose into CQDs and the application of LB-CQDs in drug release.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from cassava stems via acid/alkali-assisted hydrothermal carbonization: formation, mechanism and application in drug release

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green and sustainable carbon quantum dots from lignocellulosic biomass (LB-CQDs) have been widely used in various fields. However, the recalcitrant nature of lignocellulose leads to the low yield of LB-CQDs. In this work, we proposed an effective strategy for obtaining high yield LB-CQDs from cassava stems through acid/alkali-assist hydrothermal carbonization. The results indicated that LB-CQDs processed by HNO3 +urea (N-U-CQDs) exhibited the best fluorescence characteristics due to their smaller size and higher N content. Compared with unmodified LB-CQDs, the yield and fluorescence efficiency of N-U-CQDs increased by 36% and 80%, respectively. Meanwhile, a speculation was made on the formation route and luminescence mechanism of LB-CQDs from acid/alkali-assist hydrothermal carbonization. In addition, biomass mineral materials CaCO3 was induced by LB-CQDs to prepare carrier materials CQDs/CaCO3 for the loading and sustained-release of anticancer drug (doxorubicin, DOX). The results suggested the retained specific recognition of DOX@CQDs/CaCO3 for diseased cells. Compared to the environment simulating normal tissue with pH= 7.4, DOX@CQDs/CaCO3 exhibited faster release efficiency (52.6%, 4 h) and higher release amount (93.1%, 48 h) of loaded DOX in the environment simulating cancer cells with pH= 5.0. Therefore, it offered a novel strategy for the efficient conversion of lignocellulose into CQDs and the application of LB-CQDs in drug release.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted interest since their unexpected discovery in 2004 (Bhamore et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2023; Hu et al., 2021). CQDs are zero dimensional photoluminescence materials with the particle size less than 10 nm. Their quasi spherical structure, internally carbonized carbon nuclei, crystalline graphite structure, as well as surface functional groups, endow CQDs with various excellent performance. In particular, their fluorescence performance can be regulated by utilizing their quantum size effect and surface defect states, further expanding their application in detection, catalysis, anti-counterfeiting, and imaging fields (Wu et al., 2022; Mondal and Saha, 2019; Tyagi et al., 2020; Shinde et al., 2018). More importantly, due to their prominent characteristics such as wide preparation sources, low toxicity, and biocompatibility, they gradually replace traditional semiconductor quantum dots and play an increasingly crucial role in the main application fields of quantum dots, such as biological imaging, chemical analysis, medical fields, and optoelectronic devices (Asawaworarit et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2004; Sun et al., 2006; Youssef and El-Sayed, 2018; Lv et al., 2023).

Recently, various biomass have been used as carbon source to synthesize CQDs, such as fresh grass, cabbage, chia seeds, chitosan, lignocellulose biomass, etc. (Alam et al., 2013; Atchudan et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2021; Jones et al., 2017; Liang et al., 2016; R. Wang et al., 2019; X. Wang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021). Compared with small molecular organics, lignocellulose biomass CQDs (LB-CQDs) was limited by the low yield and quantum efficiency, as well as unclear mechanism of formation and luminescence due to the recalcitrant nature and component complexity of lignocelluloic biomass (Zhu et al., 2022). Lignocellulose is abundant, low-cost, and easily obtainable, making it potential as a raw material for biomass conversion. The rich structural types of lignocellulosic biomass, such as hydroxyl, ether and ester groups, are beneficial for the conversion efficiency of biomass. However, the complicated structure in lignocellulose, as well as entangled hemicellulose and lignin, which hinder the energy transmission in the conversion process. When converting lignocellulosic biomass into LB-CQDs, it is necessary to use modifiers or pretreatments to assist in achieving high selectivity for the required products and expanding the using efficiency of raw material. Modifier or pretreatment is usually a prerequisite and key factor for the conversion of lignocellulose to improve accessibility for dissolution or separation of certain components (Deng et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2022). Therefore, in the hydrothermal preparation of LB-CQDs, in order to possibly break physical or chemical barriers and improve the conversion efficiency of lignocellulose to LB-CQDs, the certain intervention measures must be taken (Jeong et al., 2018; Jing et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021).

Previous studies have revealed that acid/alkali pretreatment can promote the peeling of hemicellulose and lignin in amorphous region to a certain extent, and finally promote the formation of carbonization products (Markovi et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). For example, short-wavelength CQDs were prepared from the supernatant and residue using o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid assisted hydrolysis, achieving efficient utilization of biomass waste (Kumari et al., 2019). Moreover, the modifier can also passivate the surface of CQDs, change the content or type of functional groups and optimize the performance of CQDs (He et al., 2018; He et al., 2020; Masoumesh et al., 2019). After anaerobic pyrolysis, alkali pyrolysis, acidification and hydrothermal treatment of rice husks, the prepared CQDs have two fluorescence emission centers, including intrinsic and defect states (Han et al., 2020). However, LB-CQDs obtained from lignocellulosic biomass by acid/alkali combined pretreatment have not been systematically evaluated before. Thus, we should make tremendous efforts to achieve the highly efficient conversion of whole lignocellulose into CQDs by acid/alkali-assisted hydrothermal carbonization, particularly the exploration of mechanism in fluorescence and formation.

In the study, various modified LB-CQDs from cassava stems were synthesized by acid/alkali assisted hydrothermal carbonization. We systematically investigated the effects of different acid/alkali combined pretreatments on the structure and fluorescence performance of the obtained LB-CQDs, and speculated on the formation process and fluorescence mechanism of LB-CQDs. Finally, the prepared LB-CQDs were combined with the biomineral CaCO3 to obtain a drug carrier CQDs/CaCO3 composite crystal, which was used in the sustained-release field of the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) in vivo.

Cassava stem, purchased from Yunnan Province, China, underwent peeling, crushing, sieving (particle size<500 µm), and drying (90 ℃, 48 h, mass reaches constant weight) for future use. Various acid/alkali modifiers were obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory, and their specific types could be found in Table 1. Calcium chloride (CaCl2) and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) were sourced from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd., and their solutions needed to be prepared and used immediately. Mouse embryonic osteoblasts cells (MC3T3-E1) and human osteosarcoma cells (MG-63) were purchased from Guangzhou Huatuo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents used in the article were analytical pure and do not require further purification.

The synthesis of LB-CQDs followed the method used in previous work (Qiu et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022), and the simple preparation process was roughly as follows. In a polytetrafluoroethylene container, add 4 g of cassava stem, 80 mL of deionized water, and then add 0.05 M of acid/alkali modifier, respectively. After mixing all of them evenly, place them in a high-pressure reactor and react for 10 h in a 220 ℃ oven. Reaction solution passed through 0.2 µm filter membrane filtration and dialyzed in water (with a molecular weight cut off of 500 Da) for 2 days, followed by ultrasound and filtration to obtain LB-CQDs solution. Finally, the obtained LB-CQDs solution was cold dried to obtain LB-CQDs powder for subsequent testing. And the filtered residue was also dried and used for subsequent testing. The LB-CQDs without modifiers was named O-CQDs as the control group, while the other LB-CQDs with acid/alkali modifiers were named in Table 1.

Add an appropriate amount of CaCl2 to the N-U-CQDs solution (0.05 mg/mL), and then add the same concentration of Na2CO3 solution while stirring at 35 ℃. After continuous stirring for 1 h, the obtained reactants were centrifuged, washed multiple times with deionized water and ethanol, and finally vacuum dried to obtain CQDs/CaCO3 composite microspheres. Add the DOX to the solution of CQDs/CaCO3 composite microspheres, shake at a constant temperature of 37 ℃ for 1 day under dark conditions, and centrifuge to obtain DOX@CQDs/CaCO3 finally. The sustained-release test of DOX and the MTT cytotoxicity test of the carrier were shown in SI.

The morphological analysis, including TEM and SEM images, were observed using transmission electron microscopy with the specification of talos G2 200X and scanning electron microscope model JSM-IT300LV, respectively. The surface structure, including the types and contents of elements and functional groups, crystal structure, and surface charge, was determined by FTIR (Thermo Fisher Scientific), XPS (Thermo Kalpha), XRD (D/max-2500) and Zeta potential (Zetasizernano), respectively. The optical properties, including UV absorption characteristics and photoluminescence characteristics, were characterized by UV spectrophotometer (UV-2007) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7100), respectively. The calculation of quantum yields (QY) was shown in SI.

For LB-CQDs with extremely small particle sizes, their performances were greatly influenced by their microstructure (Zhang et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2022). TEM images and size statistics indicated that the addition of modifiers had a certain impact on the particle size of LB-CQDs, but no significant effect on the morphology (regular spherical shape and monodisperse distribution) and crystal structure (lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, single crystal graphite carbon lattice structure) of LB-CQDs. O-CQDs in Fig. 1a exhibited a spherical-like structure and uniformly dispersed, with an average particle size of 2.87 nm, which was smaller than that of alkali modified LB-CQDs in Fig. S1a-b, but larger than that of acid modified CQDs in Fig. S1c-j. NaOH and urea might promote the dissolution of lignin and the degree of carbonization of LB-CQDs, thereby resulting in an increase in size ultimately compared by O-CQDs. Acids could accelerate the separation of each component and further promote the hydrolysis of cellulose, resulted in a decrease in size of LB-CQDs. Among mineral acids modified LB-CQDs, N-CQDs (2.25 nm) in Fig. S1c had the smallest size, followed by P-CQDs (2.34 nm) in Fig. S1d, S-CQDs (2.38 nm) in Fig. S1e and Cl-CQDs (2.45 nm) in Fig. S1f. The difference of size might be that different functional groups lead to different degrees of hydrolysis, which in turn affect their particle size. In organic acids, the size of V-CQDs (2.8 nm) in Fig. S1i and H-CQDs (2.77 nm) in Fig. S1j were larger than that of A-CQDs (2.43 nm) in Fig. S1g and B-CQDs (2.4 nm) in Fig. S1h, indicated that there might be a certain relationship between the carbon chain length of organic acids and size of modified CQDs. However, N-U-CQDS (2.11 nm) in Fig. 1b had the smallest particle size and a more concentrated size distribution compared to other LB-CQDs, suggested that the combination of acid and alkali had a better hydrolysis effect than the two alone. The above results confirmed the facilitation of modifiers during the formation of LB-CQDs, and determined the best modification effect in the combination of HNO3 and urea.

It could be seen from the XRD in Fig. 1c that the diffraction peak position of O-CQDs was about 20°, belonged to the (002) peak of graphite. The intensity of the peak was high and sharp, indicated the high degree of graphitization of CQDs. However, the position of the diffraction peak of N-U-CQDs significantly shifted to the right to 24°, indicated that more doped N with smaller atomic radius than C was introduced into the surface of the CQDs. Moreover, the strength of the peak reduced and widened, represented an increase in the amorphous region and more defect states. In addition, they also had a weak shoulder gap at around 48°, belonged to the (100) peak of graphite, represented the highly conjugated SP2 in the carbon nucleus (Singh et al., 2022; Kasinathan et al., 2022).

The addition of modifiers had a certain impact on the functional groups of LB-CQDs from FTIR. Specifically, the peak strength at 3350 cm−1 in Fig. 1d, namely the tensile vibration of O-H/N-H, was greater in N-U-CQDs than that in O-CQDs. Moreover, the peak positions also had a certain degree of blue shift. Importantly, the strength of N-H (897 cm−1), C-N (1300 cm−1) and C-H (2900 cm−1) of U-CQDs was also apparently higher than that of O-CQDs, indicated that HNO3 and urea could significantly change the intensity of surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino, pyridine nitrogen, and methylene, which were beneficial to their fluorescence performance (Chai et al., 2019). The strength of C-N and N-H of U-CQDs was much higher than that of Na-CQDs in Fig. S2a, indicated that urea could significantly increase the nitrogen functional group content of CQDs. The appearance of new peaks C-S (576 cm−1) C-N and P = O (1000 cm−1) in Fig. S2b indicated the successful doping of functional groups containing S, N and P into surface of CQDs (Li et al., 2022b; Li et al., 2022a). In summary, the modifiers could significantly affect the intensity and the type of functional groups by introduced relevant elements, ultimately led to differences in the performance of CQDs (Wang et al., 2022; Quraishi et al., 2019).

The surface charge of CQDs not only reflects their stability in water, but also indirectly indicates changes in the types and contents of functional groups of CQDs (Wang et al., 2019b; Wang et al., 2019a; Zhao et al., 2019). All LB-CQDs were negatively charged in Fig. 1e and Fig. S3, therefore, the functional groups of LB-CQDs might contain more hydroxyl groups and other negatively charged functional groups. The absolute value of charge intensity significantly increased in HNO3, and had the highest charge intensity in HNO3 +urea (−16.59 mV), indicated the best stability and the highest hydroxyl content possibly, which was consistent with the FTIR results. The change in charge intensity of CQDs modified by different inorganic acids was inconsistent, which might be caused by differences in their acidity and introduced doping elements. The same phenomenon also occurred in organic acids. This might be related to the length of carbon chain, which meant that acetic acid and butyric acid with shorter carbon chains could possibly enhance the stability of LB-CQDs. In a nutshell, different acid/alkali had different modification on the stability of LB-CQDs, and the best effect of them was appeared in HNO3 +urea.

In Fig. S4, the morphology of the unmodified residue remained basically the same as that before hydrothermal treatment, with large particle size, smooth surfaces and no obvious fiber exposure, indicated a low degree of hydrolysis. The residue after modification was transformed into many irregular small fragments, followed by the appearance of rough and broken surfaces, exposed fibers, holes and cracks, which indicated that the addition of modifiers promoted the hydrolysis of lignocellulose biomass and improved the conversion rate of CQDs. The morphology of the residue caused by two types of alkali modifiers was similar, with slight cracks, burrs and the exposed fibers arranged in a directional manner. It was found that the degree of fragmentation and crack of LB-CQDs modified by inorganic acid was higher than that of LB-CQDs modified by organic acid, which might be caused by the more sufficient hydrolysis. The more sever fragmentation (smaller residue size and rougher surface) in LB-CQDs modified by HNO3 and H2SO4 compared to that modified by HCl and H3PO4, indicating that both were more conducive to the hydrolysis of biomass. However, the morphology caused by different organic acids was basically similar. In brief, acid/alkali could effectively promote the hydrolysis of biomass, thereby improved the conversion rate of lignocellulosic biomass. Moreover, the hydrolysis intensity was in the order as followed: acid > alkali, inorganic acid > organic acid and combination > individual.

All LB-CQDs mainly contained C and O elements, with a relatively low N element content of less than 10% in Fig. 1f and Fig. S5. A large amount of literature had shown that the doping elements, especially N elements, had a significant impact on the performance of LB-CQDs, particularly in fluorescence performance. The HNO3 +urea modified LB-CQDs had the highest N element content. Meanwhile, the content of S and P elements introduced by other inorganic acids (Fig. S5b) was also relatively high, reaching 15.07% and 8.64%, respectively, which laid the foundation for their good fluorescence performance of LB-CQDs in the future. For the O element, it could indirectly characterize the degree of hydrolysis and carbonization of the precursor. It should be noted that the O content of HNO3 +urea was lower than that of HNO3 and urea alone. Fig. 1g and Fig. S6a showed the peak energy spectrum of N1S. It could be mainly divided into two peaks, including 399.5 eV (pyridine nitrogen C-N) and 401 eV (amino nitrogen N-H). The minimum N-H content of unmodified LB-CQDs was 2.07%, while the content increased after modification and had the highest value of 32.92% in HNO3 +urea, which indicated more amino groups in that. The acids could also significantly increase the N-H content, while there was no obvious change pattern between organic and inorganic acids. The peak spectrum of O1S (Fig. 1h and Fig. S6b) was mainly divided into two peaks, namely CO at 530 eV and C-O/C-O-C at 532 eV, and the content of CO was significantly lower than that of C-O/C-O-C. Under the action of NaOH and organic acids, the content of CO also decreased. Among them, in the presence of butyric acid and valeric acid, the content of CO decreased more significantly. The content of CO only decreased under the action of H2SO4, while the content increased under the action of other inorganic acids. The peak division of C1S (Fig. 1i and Fig. S6c) was mainly divided into three peaks: C-C/CC at 284 eV, C-O at 285 eV and CO at 287 eV. Like O and N, the content of the three peaks under each modifier also varies. However, it was worth noting that under the condition of unmodified, HNO3 +urea, and valeric acid, the positions of the three peaks all shift to the left by about 2 eV. The S2p, Cl2p, N1 s and P2p of LB-CQDs modified by inorganic acid (H2SO4, HCl, HNO3 and H3PO4) was showed in Figure. S6d. The peak splitting of doping elements proved that the element had been successfully doped on the surface of LB-CQDs, and the richness of functional groups provided a basis for subsequent property, especially fluorescence performance (Wang et al., 2016).

Fluorescence was the most important optical property in LB-CQDs, and Fig. 2a and Fig. S7 summarized the fluorescence performance of LB-CQDs. The position of absorption peak in UV–vis curve did not change as the change of type and content (not displayed here) of acid/alkali. All curves had significantly stronger absorption peaks at around 282 nm, which belonged to the π-π * transition of the conjugated CC, and a weaker small shoulder peak at 225 nm, which was attributed to the n-π * transition of the CC-CO (Rodriguez-Padron et al., 2018; Ji et al., 2022). However, the intensity of the curve absorption peak varied among different modifiers. In alkalis, the addition of urea and NaOH could lead to a decrease in the absorption peak intensity at 282 nm. This might be the reason that alkali promoted the hydrolysis of lignin, thereby deepened the color of the solution. The absorption peak intensity under the single action of acid modifier and the combined action of acid+alkali modifier was both lower than that of pure LB-CQDs. The intensity varied under different modifiers, but there was no obvious change pattern to follow. In the PL spectrum, the urea increased the fluorescence intensity of LB-CQDs by 143%, because that urea might not only promote the hydrolysis of lignin and deepen the degree of carbonization, but also provide the doping of N elements to facilitate the fluorescence intensity. However, the NaOH significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of LB-CQDs. The reason might be that NaOH could promote lignin decomposition, but the strong alkalinity or the direct reaction with surface functional groups of LB-CQDs, might cause to an obvious decrease in intensity. The introduction of acid could greatly increase the fluorescence intensity of LB-CQDs, as it could promote the hydrolysis of cellulose in cassava stem and facilitate the formation of LB-CQDs. The introduction of N element in HNO3 resulted in the strongest fluorescence intensity of LB-CQDs (increase by 3.33 times), while the doping effects of S, Cl and P elements were basically similar. Organic acids could also serve as carbon sources to prepare LB-CQDs, so the appearance of dual carbon sources led to a significantly higher increase in fluorescence intensity than unmodified LB-CQDs. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of LB-CQDs under the combined action of acid and alkali was better than that of the two alone, and N-U-CQDs has the highest intensity (increase by 6.44 times) among them (strong contrast in photos).

From Fig. 2b and Fig. S8a, the quality of the residue after alkali and organic acid treatment has little change and still accounting for 70%− 77% and 68%− 80% of the raw materials respectively. However, after mineral acid treatment, the quality of the residue has significantly decreased and was only 35%− 40% of the original quality. This indicated that inorganic acids could greatly promote the hydrolysis of biomass, which might be related to the strong acidity and doped elements, consistent with SEM and XPS results. The quality of LB-CQDs had been improved, and the improvement effect of alkali modifiers (4%−5%) was better than that of acid modifiers (2%−4%), which might be related to their ability to dissolve lignin and promoted its hydrolysis. The QY also increased after the addition of acid/alkali modifiers, which was consistent with the change trend of fluorescence intensity. Among them, HNO3 +urea had the best modification effect, with the lowest residue amount (34.01%), the highest QY (8.91%) and yield (7.01%), indicated that both of them could jointly promote the hydrolysis and carbonization of biomass, and had a synergistic effect on the yield and performance of LB-CQDs. In the component analysis of the residue from Fig. 2c and Fig. S8b, it was found that the utilization rate of holocellulose and lignin also increased obviously after modification. Alkali was helpful to improve the decomposition efficiency of lignin, while acid significantly increased the hydrolysis rate of holocellulose, and the effect of inorganic acid was stronger than that of organic acid. HNO3 +urea still had the highest utilization rate for both, which was 76.17% for holocellulose and 23.51% for lignin, respectively.

A detailed analysis of fluorescence performance was conducted in N-U-CQDs. The 3D fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 2d showed that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were approximately at 372 nm and 434 nm, respectively, which was the same as results in Fig. 2a. The normalized intensity fluorescence spectra in Fig. 2e indicated that the emission behaviors were excitation wavelength dependent. As the excitation wavelength increased, the position of emission peak gradually red-shifted, which was the same as fluorescence characteristics of most biomass CQDs.

The stability of N-U-CQDs solution was also explored, including storage time (Fig. 2f), UV irradiation (Fig. 2g), NaCl concentration (Fig. 2h), and pH value (Fig. 2i). After 3 months of storage at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of N-U-CQDs remained basically unchanged. Even after a year, the fluorescence intensity of N-U-CQDs still around 88% of the original intensity, indicated high stability of N-U-CQDs at room temperature. As the UV irradiation time increased, the fluorescence intensity of N-U-CQDs gradually decreased, but the degree of decline was not significant, and the fluorescence intensity of that remained around 83%− 95% of original level. The fluorescence intensity remained basically unchanged at 96% when the NaCl concentration was less than 1.5 M. The intensity was basically maintained at about 94% when the pH= 3–11, and it only decreased when the pH< 3 and pH> 11. Meanwhile, the decrease at pH= 1 was much smaller than that at pH= 13. In summary, the fluorescence intensity of N-U-CQDs was less affected by external environmental changes and had high fluorescence stability, making it suitable for application to related areas in cells, which might attribute to the high zeta potential and abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation pathway of LB-CQDs after acid/alkali assisted hydrothermal treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above analysis and discussion of their structure and performance, a possible speculation could be made on the formation pathway and fluorescence mechanism of LB-CQDs, which was showed in Fig. 3. The formation path was that raw materials underwent dehydration and depolymerization reactions in a high-temperature and high-pressure aqueous environment, resulted in a series of small molecular fragments. These small molecular fragments underwent intermolecular and intramolecular dehydration and condensation reactions, formed a series of covalent molecules. These covalent molecules then underwent a series of reactions such as polymerization, cross-linking, and carbonization, ultimately formed LB-CQDs. In this process, acid/alkali modifiers mainly played two roles. One was equivalent to acting as an acid hydrolysis agent, assisted in the hydrolysis of biomass, promoted the removal of amorphous areas in biomass and the destruction of plant cell walls, ultimately made cellulose more easily exposed. The second was to act as a surface oxidant or heteroatom dopant to provide a large number of active sites for biomass in the process of etherification and depolymerization to generate small molecules, and to modify the surface functional groups of LB-CQDs, enrich the types and numbers of surface functional groups, and provide a basis for improving its performance, especially its fluorescence performance. It was speculated that the fluorescence source was formed by the combined action of the intrinsic state of carbon nuclei and the defect state of surface functional groups. After hydrolysis, especially under the oxidation of acid/base modifiers, the surface defects formed acted as the electron capture center, thus led to the fluorescence emission related to the surface state. The multiple energy levels caused by surface defects resulted in fluorescence emission related to the ground state and carbon nucleus state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on sustained-release of DOX assisted by LB-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The relevant morphology and structure of CaCO3, CQDs/CaCO3, DOX@CQDs/CaCO3 were shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S9. It could be seen from Fig. 4a that the CaCO3 before modification was a typical calcite structure, and the solid structure of the cube was mostly above 10 µm. The structure of CaCO3 assisted by CQDs from Fig. 4b was a spherical structure with rough and porous surface and hollow interior, with particle sizes mostly less than 5 µm. Biomineral materials with this structure could serve as ideal drug carriers. The particle size increased to a certain extent and the surface became rougher after loading the drug DOX from Fig. 4c, which indicated the successful loading of DOX. In the FTIR of Fig. S9a, the peaks of CQDs/CaCO3 and DOX@CQDs/CaCO3 were located at 1450 cm−1, 870 cm−1 and 750 cm−1, which belonged to the asymmetric stretching vibration, in-plane bending vibration, and out of plane bending vibration of CO3 2-, respectively. This was exactly the typical spherical aragonite structure of CaCO3. Meanwhile, there were characteristic functional groups belonging to CQDs at 2900 cm−1, 1640 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1, which were tensile vibrations of C-H, -CO and C-N. In the XRD of Fig. S9b, the CQDs/CaCO3 pattern peaks at 2θ of 21°, 26°, 27° and 33° corresponded to (004), (110), (112) and (114) crystal planes of typical vaterite structure of CaCO3, respectively. However, the weak diffraction peak at 29.4° simultaneously corresponded to (104) crystal plane of calcite structure of CaCO3, which indicated that most of them were vaterite structures and only a small amount of calcite structures. Similarly, there was a weak and broad diffraction peak of graphite carbon belonging to CQDs at around 24°. The data from FTIR and XRD indicated that CQDs and CaCO3 had successfully combined to form a composite crystal of CQDs/CaCO3, and the displayed structure was consistent with the results of SEM. Fig. 4d showed the fluorescence intensity corresponding to each material. It could be seen from that the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs/CaCO3 solution significantly decreased by about 35%, indirectly indicated a close binding between DOX and CaCO3. After drug loading, the fluorescence intensity of DOX@CQDs/CaCO3 decreased roughly between 15%− 20%, indicated that pH had little effect on the intensity of CQDs. The fluorescence intensity of the material at pH= 5.0 was slightly higher than that at pH= 7.4, which might be due to the pH response of biomineral materials.

Fig. 4e showed the relationship between release rate and time of simulation of in vitro drug release. Among them, the pH of 7.4 and 5.0 was used to simulate the physiological environment of normal tissues and diseased tumor tissues, respectively. From the graph, it could be seen that DOX reached the release equilibrium after approximately 10 h. There was also a significant pH response, where the release rate at pH 5.0 was much higher than the release efficiency at pH 7.4. It was speculated that the release process was the rapid release process of the drug in the first 4 h, with release efficiencies of 7.2% and 52.6% at pH 7.4 and 5.0, respectively. Subsequently, the drug was slowly released until the release equilibrium was basically reached, and the final release efficiency at 48 h was 13.9% and 93.1%, respectively. The pH responsive release phenomenon might be due to the external acidic environment at pH= 5.0, which led to the destruction of the carrier microsphere structure and ultimately caused to the rapid release of DOX drugs. The structure of carrier sphere was relatively completed at pH= 7.4, leading to slow release of DOX, especially internal DOX. Meanwhile, the final lower release efficiency could effectively reduce the toxic side effects of drugs on normal histiocyte. In summary, the carrier structure prepared in this article could achieve the goal of drug sustained release, and had certain specific recognition for normal and diseased tissues, greatly reducing toxic side effects. Besides, compared to other biomineral materials of the same type, the release efficiency of DOX has also been improved to a certain extent, and due to the fluorescence performance of CQDs, it can achieve the function of intracellular fluorescence labeling (Li et al., 2016; Li et al., 2021).

As a drug release carrier in the body, the most basic performance was the non-toxic nature, in addition to meeting the purpose of drug sustained release. Fig. 4f showed two types of cells selected for MTT cytotoxicity experiment to detect the toxicity of each material. Three materials, including CQDs, CaCO3, and CQDS/CaCO3, all had high cell survival rates for two different types of cells. For example, when the concentration reached 400 μg/mL, there was still more than 90% cell activity. Due to the introduction of DOX, DOX@CQDs /CaCO3 had a lower cell survival rate compared to the other three materials. MTT experiments had shown that the prepared CQDs/CaCO3 had extremely low cytotoxicity, which could be used as potential drug carrier materials for drug delivery systems in vivo. Based on the previous analysis, it was speculated that the release process of the drug in the tissue was shown in Fig. 4g.

In summary, LB-CQDs could meet the requirements of inducing biomineral materials as drug carriers, and its non-toxic, high fluorescence intensity and stability made it possible to target fluorescence labeling in cellular tissues.

We report an effective method for obtaining LB-CQDs from cassava stems through acid/alkali-assist hydrothermal treatment. By comparing and analyzing the morphology, structure, and optical properties of different LB-CQDs prepared, the following conclusions could be drawn. The strong acidity of inorganic acids significantly promotes the hydrolysis of cellulose and improves the yield of LB-CQDs. Meanwhile, the various doping elements, especially N element, could greatly improve the QY of LB-CQDs. The presence of organic acids enhanced the yield of LB-CQDs through the action of dual carbon sources. The synergistic effect of HNO3 +urea combination provided the best fluorescence performance of LB-CQDs. More importantly, it was successfully applied to the loading and sustained-release of anticancer drugs (doxorubicin, DOX) by combining U-N-CQDs with CaCO3 to prepare biomass mineral drug carriers.

ce:section.ce:para: Calculation of quantum yield; release testing and calculation of DOX; MTT testing; TEM of CQDs modified by acid/alkali; FTIR of CQDs modified by acid/alkali; zeta potential of CQDs modified by acid/alkali; SEM images of residue of CQDs modified by acid/alkali; XPS of CQDs modified by acid/alkali; N1S, O1S and C1S of CQDs modified by acid/alkali. UV absorption and fluorescence properties of CQDs modified by acid/alkali; the statistical chart of QY, yield, residue of CQDs and the utilization rate of lignin and holocellulose of CQDs; FTIR (a) and XRD (b) of related materials.
